pages.full_text:
The tide is out during lunch - huge mud flats + marinas. The houses are modern + expensive, or old + expensive. Some lovely gardens, but many with no gardens which shows they must be holiday houses. A prosperous area. Mostly French people.
Everytime we tried to set out along the footpath it seemed to wind back to the sea. Then we were distracted by the French air force practising their Red Arrows. Amazing.
   So we gave up on the walk + went back to the van.
Symbols on the     [glued-in Pavé breton coffee biscuit packet]
Breton Flag
coffee biscuit.
(black + white quartered)
Alot of the church spires are cheap-looking. Even the modern houses have huge stone surrounds to doors + windows which makes them look nice + fit in. Lots of ever-green trees, hydrangeas, agapanthus in gardens + globe artichokes in fields.
photo_captions: Pavé breton (coffee biscuit wrapper — black + white quartered, Symbols on the Breton Flag)
pages.notes: No handwritten page number visible on this page. The glued-in item is a 'Pavé breton' branded coffee biscuit packet/wrapper with colourful Breton-figure illustrations. The writer notes it as an example of 'Symbols on the Breton Flag'. Context is clearly Brittany, France. No explicit date on this page; year inferred from surrounding pages in volume B (2003–2019) but cannot be pinned without adjacent pages.

pages.full_text:
We camped near [?Six-Pleeter] ([?al Gieves])
The name of the campsite owner is Gwenaelle Thomas. It must be a Breton name. In 1950 2 mill people spoke Breton. Now its only 100,000. It is the only Celtic language not supported by the gov'. It only cost €8.80 + is a lovely campsite.
__Camping à la ferme__   * * * * *   So dark at night that Simon saw a shooting star + opened his constellations app on his mob. Very friendly woman who gave us a bag of veg for free. We had a delicious supper with pâté, cheese + choc. + almond croissants
   Sunday
Simon took ages to fill the van with the presser tap.
New German word - Grablegung - The Entombment
We drove to look at Enclosed Parishes, peculiar to this region, made possible by wealth from the linen trade 16 17 18[C]. Like cathedral closes, the parish churches + bone chapels were enclosed by a wall.
pages.notes: Top line reads 'We camped near [?Six-Pleeter] ([?al Gieves])' — the place name is unclear. Could be a Breton village; possible readings include 'Six-Pletter', 'Six-Pleeter', or another Breton place name near a coastal area. The text in parentheses after the place name is also unclear — could be 'al Gieves', 'al Bieves', or a Breton sub-locality name. Campsite owner name 'Gwenaelle Thomas' is clearly written. 'Camping à la ferme' is underlined. 'Grablegung' is a German word glossed as 'The Entombment'. 'Enclosed Parishes' refers to Breton 'Enclos paroissiaux'. The year cannot be confirmed from this page alone; context is Brittany, France.
